channel.title: The Grant Desk - Cross-disciplinary & Social Impact
channel.description: Paid open calls, fellowships and residencies in AI, tech, research, and digital and mixed-media arts, sorted into the right pile. Updated as new calls land on the desk. Filtered to: Cross-disciplinary & Social Impact.

item.title: Beca Plataforma Bogota: Arte, Ciencia y Tecnologia 2026 (Colombia residents only)
item.description:
Organisation: Idartes / Plataforma Bogota

Location: Bogota, Colombia. Colombian citizens or foreigners legally resident in Bogota only.

Award: 4 grants of COP 12,000,000 each (approx. EUR 2,700) to run interactive art + science + technology labs at Plataforma Bogota. No application fee.

Duration: Labs run at Plataforma Bogota, the city's art-science-technology media lab.

Deadline: 24 Aug 2026

Plataforma Bogota, Idartes' art, science and technology media lab, awards four grants of COP 12,000,000 each to run interactive labs combining art, science, and technology. ELIGIBILITY: Colombian citizens or foreigners legally resident in Bogota only. Deadline 24 August 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: FUJIFILM GFX Challenge Grant Program 2026
item.description:
Organisation: FUJIFILM

Location: Open to residents of the countries/regions listed under Entry Submission on the program page. Final content showcased on fujifilm-x.com plus planned exhibitions in various countries.

Award: 5 Global Grant Awards of USD 10,000 and 10 Regional Grant Awards of USD 5,000. All recipients also get complimentary use of a GFX System camera body (including the FUJIFILM GFX ETERNA 55 filmmaking camera) and two GF lenses for the duration of the project, plus mentoring and technical assistance from Fujifilm technicians and product experts. Total approximate retail value of all awards: USD 229,500. No purchase necessary; no entry fee.

Duration: Application window 16 June to 17 August 2026. Production period concludes in May 2027, after which final content is showcased on fujifilm-x.com and in a planned series of exhibitions.

Deadline: 17 Aug 2026

The GFX Challenge Grant Program, sponsored by FUJIFILM and running since 2021, awards 5 Global Grant Awards (USD 10,000) and 10 Regional Grant Awards (USD 5,000) to help aspiring creatives bring imaging projects to life. It is designed to nurture emerging and promising content creators, giving them the opportunity to create content on topics with significant meaning to them while gaining experience with FUJIFILM GFX System gear, including the GFX ETERNA 55 filmmaking camera. Proposed projects may be submitted as still photography or in movie format. AWARDS: both tiers include complimentary use of a GFX System camera body and two GF lenses for the duration of the project, plus mentoring and technical assistance from Fujifilm technicians and product experts. At the end of the production period in May 2027, recipients' final content is showcased on fujifilm-x.com, with a series of exhibitions planned in various countries. ELIGIBILITY: open to both amateur and professional photographers and videographers who are residents of the countries/regions listed in the Entry Submission section of the program page; not open to employees or officers of FUJIFILM and its subsidiaries. TO APPLY: combine three items into a single PDF: (1) the preformatted cover letter (downloadable from the program page); (2) the project proposal covering descriptive plan, objectives, evaluation and outcome measures, communications, how you will use the Fujifilm partnership and mentoring, schedule, and budget; (3) a portfolio for each individual involved. Proposals must be in English (optionally accompanied by a version in your own language; language proficiency is not judged). Entry period 16 June to 17 August 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Stichting DOEN: Funding for Pioneering Culture and Social Initiatives (rolling, Netherlands)
item.description:
Organisation: Stichting DOEN

Location: Netherlands-based fund (backed by the Dutch charity lotteries); themes have differing geographic focus areas. Legal entities only.

Award: No fixed amounts published; funding is tailored per initiative. No application fee.

Duration: Apply at least four months before the project start date; the application procedure takes about four months.

Deadline: Rolling / undated

Stichting DOEN, funded by the Dutch charity lotteries, supports pioneering initiatives within themes including the transformative power of imagination (art and culture), regenerative economy and solidarity economy. Applications are accepted year-round via the Mijn DOEN portal from legal entities only (no individuals) and must be received at least four months before the project starts; the assessment procedure takes about four months. Amateur art practice and standalone productions of films, books, CDs or websites are not supported. No application fee.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Nebraska Arts Council: Individual Artist Fellowship 2026 (Performing Arts)
item.description:
Organisation: Nebraska Arts Council

Location: Nebraska, USA. Applicants must have been Nebraska residents for at least 24 months before applying and remain residents during the fellowship year.

Award: Up to $5,000 for a one-year fellowship supporting professional development, including the creation of new art, presentations, training and/or research. No application fee.

Duration: One-year fellowship period

Deadline: 17 Nov 2026

The Nebraska Arts Council's Individual Artist Fellowship awards up to $5,000 for a one-year period to support professional development: creating new work, presentations, training and/or research. The fellowship rotates among three umbrella categories; the 2026 round accepts Performing Arts applications in theater/playwriting, choreography, filmmaking/screenwriting, and music/sound. ELIGIBILITY: Nebraska resident for at least 24 months before application, remaining resident during the fellowship year (attestation required); 19 or older; not enrolled in an undergraduate program in the discipline applied for (graduate and certificate programs allowed); applicants must be the originator of the submitted work, not an interpreter. Previous winners may reapply after three years with new work if prior grant requirements were completed. SUBMISSION: artist biography, artist statement (name redacted; themes, influences, techniques), and five work samples with descriptions or synopses. Questions: meagan.dion@nebraska.gov. DEADLINE: 17 November 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Night as Commons and the Dark Side of Light: Residency (Transylvania)
item.description:
Organisation: Hypha etc.

Location: Campu Cetatii, Transylvania, Romania. For artists based in Creative Europe countries except Romania (Netherlands eligible).

Award: EUR 30/day allowance, plus accommodation and travel (EUR 400 under 5,000 km; EUR 800 over), with green mobility, family, and accessibility top-ups. EU-funded. 6 weeks. No application fee.

Duration: Residency 1 October to 10 November 2026.

Deadline: 01 Aug 2026

Hypha etc. hosts a six-week EU-funded residency (1 October to 10 November 2026) in Campu Cetatii, Transylvania, on the theme Night as Commons and the Dark Side of Light: night, darkness, and light pollution, a strong fit for media and installation practices. Support: EUR 30/day allowance, accommodation, travel allowance (EUR 400 under 5,000 km, EUR 800 over), plus green mobility, family, and accessibility top-ups. ELIGIBILITY: artists based in Creative Europe countries except Romania. Deadline 1 August 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Medialab Matadero: Situated Research Residencies 2027 (Madrid)
item.description:
Organisation: Medialab Matadero

Location: Madrid, Spain. In-person residency; open internationally to individuals and collectives.

Award: EUR 10,000 per project (before taxes) for three selected projects, plus individual workspace, Fablab and technical materials, curatorial guidance, and logistical and financial support for public activities. No application fee.

Duration: January to July 2027; cannot be carried out remotely, with extended stays in Madrid recommended especially March to June. Live online Q&A on 8 September 2026, 6pm CEST.

Deadline: 27 Sep 2026

Medialab Matadero in Madrid selects three projects for a research and creation residency bringing together contemporary art, design, science, technology and society, welcoming critical, experimental and collaborative work. Each project must connect clearly with one or more themes from Medialab's recent programs: LAB 1 Sentient Media, LAB 2 The Metabolic Sublime, LAB 3 Synthetic Minds, LAB 4 Weird Futures and LAB 5 The Mechanics of Truth. Open to individuals and collectives from any discipline (design, art, architecture, film, journalism, digital media, environmental science, philosophy, engineering, programming, activism and more). SUPPORT: EUR 10,000 per project (before taxes), individual workspace, access to the Fablab and technical materials, curatorial team guidance, and logistical and financial support for public activities; proposals must include public engagement plans and produce documentation. RESIDENCY: January to July 2027, in person in Madrid. DEADLINE: 27 September 2026. No application fee.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: British Council Ireland: UK-IRL Culture Connects Grant 2026/27
item.description:
Organisation: British Council Ireland / Arts Council Ireland

Location: Ireland and the UK. For bilateral partnerships of one individual based in Ireland and one based in England, Northern Ireland, Scotland or Wales; in-person collaboration required.

Award: Up to EUR 3,000 per collaboration (smaller amounts also eligible), covering artist fees (including your own time), collaborator fees, travel and accommodation, studio or space hire, materials, and access costs including disability support. No application fee.

Duration: Activity between October 2026 and March 2027; must be completed by 31 March 2027

Deadline: 31 Jul 2026

The UK-IRL Culture Connects Grant 2026/27 is a British Council Ireland initiative delivered in association with Arts Council Ireland, supporting the early stages of artistic collaboration between Ireland and the four UK nations through in-person exchange. It prioritises creative development over final outputs: research and development of new ideas, mentorship, exploring collaborative pathways with producers or partners, experimenting with new technologies or practices, and developing initiatives that support creative exchange. WHO: applications are made by individual artists or creative practitioners working as a bilateral partnership of one individual based in Ireland and one based in England, Northern Ireland, Scotland or Wales; applicants must be resident in Ireland or the UK for the project duration, have a proven professional track record, be at an early or developing career stage, and not be in full-time education. One application per collaboration. FUNDING: up to EUR 3,000 covering artist and collaborator fees, travel and accommodation, studio hire, materials, and access costs. Activities must involve in-person collaboration (fully remote projects are not eligible) and take place between October 2026 and 31 March 2027. Apply via the British Council GoodGrants platform; an information webinar recording and overview document are on the programme page. DEADLINE: 31 July 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: OUTsider Fest 2027: Dreaming US into Being (call for queer time-based art)
item.description:
Organisation: OUTsider

Location: Austin, Texas, United States (festival February 2027). International-friendly; prioritizes marginalized artists including by migratory status and nationality.

Award: Festival presentation slot for time-based work (film, performance, sound, multimedia). Artist compensation and travel support not stated. No fee stated.

Duration: Festival February 2027.

Deadline: Closed 20 Jul 2026

OUTsider, the transdisciplinary queer arts festival in Austin, invites LGBTQ+ artists working in any time-based form (film, performance, sound, multimedia) to propose work for its 2027 edition themed Dreaming US into Being. The call prioritizes marginalized artists, explicitly including by migratory status and nationality. Deadline 20 July 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Research Trip to Switzerland
item.description:
Organisation: Pro Helvetia (Swiss Arts Council)

Location: Two- to four-week research trip to one or more regions in Switzerland. Open to applicants based in Pro Helvetia's liaison-office regions (Arab region, East Asia, South America, South Asia and Vietnam, and West, Central, East and Southern Africa).

Award: Accommodation, travel costs, and daily allowances covered, plus a local coach (a cultural professional from the Swiss artistic field) to facilitate networking and exchange. Applicants with childcare responsibilities can request up to CHF 1,000 per month toward childcare or children's travel expenses. No application fee.

Duration: Two- to four-week research trip (at least two weeks encouraged for environmental reasons); earliest start three months after application.

Deadline: Rolling / undated

Pro Helvetia offers a two- to four-week research trip to one or more regions in Switzerland for cultural practitioners from the regions of its liaison offices: the Arab region, East Asia, South America, South Asia and Vietnam, and West, Central, East and Southern Africa. Applicants active in any of the disciplines supported by Pro Helvetia are welcome, and applications from both individuals and artist duos are accepted. SELECTION CRITERIA: permanent residence in one of the liaison-office regions and active participation in the respective cultural scene; a comprehensive portfolio and proven track record of professional activity in the applicant's field; strong professional interest in the selected destination connected to the applicant's artistic practice and to networking with the local cultural field; a concrete research topic that can deepen exchange between the cultural scenes of Switzerland and the applicant's country of residence; and knowledge of English or one of the Swiss languages. For environmental reasons, applicants are asked to consider a travel duration of at least two weeks. WHAT IS OFFERED: accommodation, travel costs, and daily allowances, plus a coach (a cultural professional from the local artistic field) to facilitate networking and exchange; artists with childcare responsibilities can apply for up to CHF 1,000 per month toward childcare or children's travel expenses (a budget must be presented in advance). EXCLUSIONS: applicants from countries outside the Pro Helvetia liaison offices' area of responsibility may not apply, and Pro Helvetia cannot consider requests from applicants enrolled in a school, university curriculum, basic training, or education programme. APPLICATION: submit a title and brief description of the proposal; a motivation explaining the professional interest and artistic research in the selected destination; the desired length of stay and destination; and a CV and portfolio. All applications must be submitted in English. KEY DATES: submissions accepted year-round; notification of decision eight weeks after submitting; earliest project start three months after application. Pro Helvetia assumes no responsibility for the approval of visa applications. Apply via the Pro Helvetia application portal.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Rijkscultuurfondsen: Interdisciplinary Grant
item.description:
Organisation: Dutch national cultural funds (Rijkscultuurfondsen), via the Mondriaan Fund

Location: Netherlands. For professional makers and organisations based in the Netherlands or the Caribbean part of the Kingdom whose practice spans multiple disciplines.

Award: EUR 12,500 to 50,000 per project. The individual-maker track is approximately EUR 24,900 for 12 months (income cap EUR 55,000 including the grant).

Duration: Up to 12 months for the individual-maker track.

Deadline: 01 Oct 2026

The Interdisciplinary grant is a new joint scheme of the six Dutch national cultural funds (Mondriaan Fund, Performing Arts Fund NL, Fund for Cultural Participation, Netherlands Film Fund, Creative Industries Fund NL, and the Dutch Foundation for Literature), announced on 7 July 2026. It supports makers and organisations whose work genuinely crosses the boundaries of the individual funds' disciplines (visual art, design, film, literature, performing arts, and cultural participation) and cannot be contained within a single discipline. TWO TRACKS: an Individual track for professional makers with a demonstrable interdisciplinary practice (at least one year of experience; income cap EUR 55,000 including the grant; approximately EUR 24,900 for 12 months); and an Organisation track for organisations running multi-discipline projects. AMOUNT: EUR 12,500 to 50,000 per project. ELIGIBILITY: makers and organisations based in the Netherlands or the Caribbean part of the Kingdom. APPLYING: handled through the Mondriaan Fund's online application portal (info@mondriaanfonds.nl); an account is required in advance. DEADLINE: 1 October 2026, 16:00 Dutch time (single application round).

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Global Artivism: Governance Fellow
item.description:
Organisation: Global Artivism (a fiscally sponsored project of Res Publica US Inc.)

Location: Remote, open globally. Priority given to applicants from the Global South.

Award: USD 1,200 to 1,800 per month (independent contractor fee), paid on receipt of monthly invoices. No employee benefits, paid leave, or insurance; contractor covers their own taxes and compliance.

Duration: 12-month independent contractor engagement from August 2026, with potential renewal subject to funding and mutual agreement. Flexible hours with scheduled calls across time zones.

Deadline: Closed 16 Jul 2026

Global Artivism, a Global Majority-led movement that engages the power of arts and culture toward a just world on a safe planet, is contracting a Governance Fellow for a mentored, time-limited engagement focused on governance systems, democratic institutional design, and Global Majority-led movement infrastructure. The contractor supports the development and maintenance of Global Artivism's governance architecture, including documentation, committee coordination, and research that supports the Democratic Transition process, and is expected to take initiative on assigned tasks and grow through structured mentorship. The movement is governed by a Global Council with a Movement Secretariat coordination team; the Fellow coordinates primarily with the Governance Lead alongside the Operations Director, Secretariat members, and Council committees (coordination relationships do not constitute supervisory authority). KEY RESPONSIBILITIES: governance documentation (frameworks, committee charters, terms of reference, process documentation; maintaining an organized, current, consistently formatted document library; preparing materials for Global Council meetings); committee coordination (scheduling, materials, meeting notes, follow-up, trackers of decisions and action items, agendas and briefing packs); Democratic Transition and electoral process support (research on governance models, electoral processes, and democratic participation mechanisms; drafting materials for the Democratic Transition Roadmap 2025-2027; preparing electoral framework and testing documentation; tracking milestones); and governance research (comparative analyses and briefing notes; staying current on distributed governance, participatory democracy, and decentralized leadership; contributing to knowledge products). REQUIRED: manages multiple documentation tasks and meets deadlines with attention to accuracy and formatting (experience via union organizing, participatory facilitation, cooperative governance, or community-based decision-making counts alongside academic or formal work history); good written English; ability to take initiative and work independently; genuine interest in governance, movement building, or institutional design; ability to operate as an independent contractor; and commitment to Global Artivism's values. PREFERRED: background in governance, law, political science, or international development; interest in Global Majority leadership and distributed power; civil society/NGO/movement experience; familiarity with arts, culture, or social justice movements; multilingual ability in languages relevant to Global South contexts. This is an independent contractor engagement managed through Global Artivism's fiscal sponsorship with Res Publica US Inc.; the contractor sets their own hours and methods, may serve other clients, and handles their own taxes and local compliance. TO APPLY: submit an expression of interest via the ABM Intelligence application page; submissions are reviewed on a rolling basis by ABM's consulting team. Expressions of interest close Thursday 16 July 2026, 10pm MYT (UTC+8); late submissions will not be reviewed. Priority is given to candidates from the Global South and Global Majority communities, and community organizing, grassroots coordination, and self-taught practice are valued alongside formal credentials.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Global Artivism: Operations Fellow
item.description:
Organisation: Global Artivism (a fiscally sponsored project of Res Publica US Inc.)

Location: Remote, open globally. Priority given to applicants from the Global South.

Award: USD 1,000 to 1,500 per month (independent contractor fee), paid on receipt of monthly invoices. No employee benefits, paid leave, or insurance; contractor covers their own taxes and compliance.

Duration: 12-month independent contractor engagement from August 2026, with potential renewal subject to funding and mutual agreement. Flexible hours with scheduled calls across time zones.

Deadline: Closed 16 Jul 2026

Global Artivism, a Global Majority-led movement that engages the power of arts and culture toward a just world on a safe planet, is contracting an Operations Fellow for a mentored, time-limited engagement focused on organizational infrastructure and systems design. Working alongside the Operations Director, the contractor designs operational frameworks spanning financial workflows, contractor management, governance alignment, and onboarding, contributing directly to infrastructure decisions while building practical experience in how a Global Majority-led movement organization functions operationally. The Fellow coordinates primarily with the Operations Director alongside the Governance Lead, Project Manager, Admin Coordinator, and Secretariat members (coordination relationships do not constitute supervisory authority; decision-making is distributed through governance structures and committees). KEY RESPONSIBILITIES: infrastructure design and framework development (designing and documenting operational frameworks; contributing to staffing models, organizational structures, and resource frameworks that enable both centralized and decentralized work; designing onboarding and operational guidance; aligning operational systems with governance frameworks and democratic decision-making); systems and knowledge documentation (operational guidance and toolkits for distributed teams; internal knowledge systems such as documentation, checklists, and decision trees); and capacity building and research (identifying where documentation or systems redesign enables teams to work more independently; flagging operational bottlenecks and proposing solutions; sharing learning with Council, committees, and teams; researching operational models relevant to Global Artivism's structure). REQUIRED: manages multiple tasks and meets deadlines with attention to detail and comfort coordinating logistics across a distributed team (experience via coordinating community events, grassroots logistics, or operations in any context counts alongside formal work history); good written English; comfort with digital tools (Google Workspace, Slack, or similar); genuine interest in operations, organizational systems, or movement infrastructure; ability to operate as an independent contractor; and commitment to Global Artivism's values. PREFERRED: background in business administration, project management, or finance; interest in international civil society, movement building, or Global Majority leadership; civil society/NGO/community experience; familiarity with digital project management or operations tools; multilingual ability in languages relevant to Global South contexts. This is an independent contractor engagement managed through Global Artivism's fiscal sponsorship with Res Publica US Inc.; the contractor sets their own hours and methods, may serve other clients, and handles their own taxes and local compliance. TO APPLY: submit an expression of interest via the ABM Intelligence application page; submissions are reviewed on a rolling basis by ABM's consulting team. Expressions of interest close Thursday 16 July 2026, 10pm MYT (UTC+8); late submissions will not be reviewed. Priority is given to candidates from the Global South and Global Majority communities, and community organizing, grassroots coordination, and self-taught practice are valued alongside formal credentials.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: EFFEA Call #5: European Festivals Fund for Emerging Artists
item.description:
Organisation: European Festivals Association (EFA)

Location: Across eligible European countries; residencies built around partner festivals registered on FestivalFinder.eu

Award: Per-residency grants: EUR 8,000 (Discovery format) or EUR 15,000 (Springboard format)

Duration: Residencies take place in 2027; call open 1 September to 3 November 2026, full guidelines published 1 September 2026

Deadline: 03 Nov 2026

The European Festivals Fund for Emerging Artists (EFFEA), an initiative of the European Festivals Association co-funded by the European Union, will open its fifth call from 1 September to 3 November 2026, with full guidelines and application details published on 1 September 2026. Call #5 will again support around 50 cross-border co-production residencies taking place in 2027, each bringing together at least three festivals from different eligible countries to accompany an emerging artist or collective. EFFEA is a co-production residency scheme: festivals join forces across borders to support an emerging artist through a process of creation and presentation, providing dedicated time, space, and tailored mentoring, exchange, and collaboration, moving beyond a single invitation toward a shared artistic development journey. Each supported project is built around one leading festival working with at least two partner festivals based in different eligible countries; one emerging artist or collective at the centre; a combination of artistic development, mentoring, exchange, and audience engagement based on the artist's needs; and presentations or outcomes shared across the partner festivals. Two residency formats are offered. DISCOVERY (EUR 8,000 grant): the proposal must include a workshop (live or online) hosted by the leading festival with at least one programmer from each partner festival, and a work-in-progress presentation at the leading festival. SPRINGBOARD (EUR 15,000 grant): the proposal must include final presentations or production results shown within each festival (minimum the leading festival plus two partners) and a live masterclass led by the artist(s) at each festival (minimum the leading festival plus two partners). WHO CAN APPLY: festivals that have existed for at least one edition and are registered on FestivalFinder.eu; the festivals apply on behalf of the residency project. Residency projects must fall within these artistic fields: architecture, ballet, contemporary dance, circus, design/applied arts, digital arts, film, folklore and folk arts, heritage, interdisciplinary, literature, music, opera, street art, theatre, photography/video, puppetry, sculpture, painting. A series of EFFEA info, networking, and Q&A sessions runs on Tuesdays 11:00-12:00 CEST (15 September, 29 September, 13 October, and 20 October 2026) to help applicants navigate the process and find partners. Across Calls #1 to #4, EFFEA has supported 619 festivals and 200 artists/collectives across Europe and beyond.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: IIAS: Asia in the World Fellowship Programme 2027-2028
item.description:
Organisation: International Institute for Asian Studies (IIAS), Leiden University

Location: Two phases: a partner institution in Asia, Africa, Latin America, or Europe (Phase 1), then Leiden, Netherlands (Phase 2). Open internationally.

Award: Leiden phase: EUR 1,250 per month stipend plus EUR 1,200 per month housing allowance, plus a travel grant; the Phase 1 stipend at the co-host institution is set by that institution. No application fee.

Duration: 9 months (two phases)

Deadline: 10 Sep 2026

The Asia in the World Fellowship Programme, run by the International Institute for Asian Studies (IIAS) at Leiden University, is a nine-month, two-phase fellowship pairing a residency at a partner institution in Asia, Africa, Latin America, or Europe (Phase 1) with six months in residence at IIAS in Leiden, the Netherlands (Phase 2). The programme supports interdisciplinary, humanities- and social-science-based research engaging Asia and its connections with the wider world. SUPPORT during the Leiden phase: EUR 1,250 per month stipend, EUR 1,200 per month housing allowance, and a travel grant; the Phase 1 stipend is provided by the co-host institution. ELIGIBILITY: scholars and researchers worldwide; open internationally. APPLICATIONS open 1 July 2026; deadline 10 September 2026. Apply via the IIAS fellowships page. NOTE: dates confirmed via secondary sources at time of entry (the funder page was not directly reachable); verify before applying.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Ucross General Residency (All Disciplines): Spring 2027
item.description:
Organisation: Ucross Foundation

Location: Sheridan, Wyoming, USA

Award: No charge for the residency; living accommodations, meals, and work space provided. USD 40 application fee.

Duration: Spring session (February through early June 2027); residencies range from two to six weeks

Deadline: Closed 15 Jul 2026

Ucross offers fully funded artist residencies on a working cattle ranch in the foothills of Wyoming's Bighorn Mountains, providing uninterrupted time and space free from the pressures and distractions of daily life. Founded in 1981 with its first residency period in 1983, Ucross hosts approximately 115 individuals per year. Residencies range from two to six weeks; at any one time up to ten people are in residence, typically four writers, four visual artists, and two composers. Each artist receives living accommodations, meals, and work space at no charge. Lunches and dinners are prepared Monday to Friday by a professional chef (lunches delivered to studios, group dinners at 6 p.m.), with ample provisions for breakfasts and weekends; towels, linens, and weekly housekeeping are provided, and there is cell service and wireless internet throughout. Residents provide their own working materials and arrange their own travel to Sheridan, Wyoming. The program is open to visual artists, writers, composers, choreographers, interdisciplinary artists, performance artists, and collaborative teams; applicants must exhibit professional standing in their field, and both established and emerging artists are encouraged to apply. Applications are submitted by discipline category: dance, music/sound, performance, film/video, playwriting/screenwriting, visual art, fiction, nonfiction, poetry, or interdisciplinary. The work sample and project proposal are the most significant features of the application and should correspond to one another; if your work is in a single discipline, select the category that best fits your project, and apply under interdisciplinary only if the project genuinely combines multiple disciplines. Work-sample requirements by category: DANCE, 2-3 video files (each up to 8 minutes and 200 MB, or up to 3 Vimeo links, with a timestamp if longer than 8 minutes); MUSIC/SOUND, 3-5 audio files (each up to 8 minutes and 200 MB, or up to 3 Vimeo links; musical scores welcome); PERFORMANCE and FILM/VIDEO, 1-3 video files (each up to 5 minutes and under 200 MB, or up to 3 Vimeo/YouTube links, with a timestamp if longer than 5 minutes); PLAYWRITING/SCREENWRITING, one complete play double-spaced noting the 20-page section to read; VISUAL ART, 10 images at 4x6 inches and 300 dpi (JPG or TIF, no PDFs) plus a data sheet listing title, medium, size, and year; FICTION and NONFICTION, 20 pages of double-spaced writing; POETRY, 6-10 poems (10 maximum) in one PDF, each poem starting on a new page, total not exceeding 15 pages; INTERDISCIPLINARY, samples representing the genres you plan to work in, combining any of the above. All written work samples must be in English. There is a USD 40 application fee. The open call for the Spring 2027 session is open May 1 to July 15, 2026 (apply by 11:59 p.m. MT on July 15). Dedicated Fellowships for Native American Visual Artists and Writers and a separate Culinary Residency are also offered.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Ecoslay Mag: Open Call for Contributions, Issue 02 "The Urban Issue"
item.description:
Organisation: Ecoslay Mag

Location: Remote / international. Two online workshops with the editorial team; contributions delivered remotely.

Award: Financial compensation for selected contributions (amount unspecified). Editorial support, proofreading, and graphic design provided. No application fee.

Duration: Contributions developed approx. 1 September 2026 to 10 January 2027, with two online workshops (first around 15 September 2026). Final contribution deadline approx. 10 January 2027.

Deadline: Closed 13 Jul 2026

Ecoslay Mag invites contributions for the second print issue of Ecoslay, "The Urban Issue," exploring the city as a space where intersectional ecological futures are negotiated, contested, resisted, and reinvented. In the Ecoslay way, pop culture and its influence are analysed and hijacked to communicate socio-ecological issues and ignite systemic change: what happens when climate politics meets internet culture, urban ecology meets reality TV, and sustainability discourse collides with camp, irony, humour, and memes. FORMATS (including but not limited to): essays, interviews, science communication, poems, visual art, documentation of (art)works, and fiction. TOPICS: urban climate communication through humour, memes, and pop culture; greenwashing, eco-branding, and sustainable aesthetics in urban contexts; urban farming, more-than-human life, housing crises, displacement and green gentrification, class and the city, maintenance and care work in relation to urbanism, urban environmental justice, and community organizing, and how these relate to pop culture; and reflections on what Ecoslay means in an age of overlapping crises. COMMITMENT: selected participants deliver one contribution to Issue 02 and attend two online workshops with the editorial team and fellow contributors for communal exchange and feedback on proposals and works-in-progress. WHAT'S OFFERED: financial compensation for selected contributions, editorial support, and edition, proofreading, and graphic design handled by the team. TIMELINE: open call closes 13 July 2026; selected contributors announced approx. 15 August 2026; work on contributions approx. 1 September 2026 to 10 January 2027; contributor workshop approx. 15 September 2026; final contribution deadline approx. 10 January 2027. HOW TO APPLY: submit via the Google form. Questions via ecoslay@protonmail.com or Instagram DM @ecoslay_mag.

Open call details

item.title: Chornobyl40: Open Call 2026 (Virtual Residency & International Exhibition)
item.description:
Organisation: Chornobyl40 (gate22 / European Institute of Chornobyl consortium)

Location: Online residency (weekly sessions Tuesdays 14:00-18:00 CET); European touring exhibition 2027 in Madrid, Berlin, Toulouse, and Kyiv. Open internationally.

Award: Residency is free to attend. For the 20 projects selected for production: EUR 500 grant for fully digital works or EUR 1,000 grant for installations requiring physical materials, plus inclusion in the international exhibition. Travel, accommodation, and transport are not covered. No application fee.

Duration: Phase 1 virtual residency September-October 2026 (weekly Tuesday sessions, 30 hours total, minimum 90% attendance). Phase 2 selection and production. Phase 3 European touring exhibition in 2027.

Deadline: Closed 12 Jul 2026

Chornobyl40 is a European artistic program, funded by the European Union, exploring the multiple legacies of the Chornobyl disaster not only as a past event but as an ongoing condition shaping the present and future. The open call invites artists, designers, researchers, collectives, and interdisciplinary/hybrid practitioners to engage with Chornobyl as a landscape, a memory, a political reality, and a speculative horizon. This is a call for interpretation, friction, and critical imagination, not illustration. STRUCTURE (three phases): PHASE 1, VIRTUAL RESIDENCY: 40 selected participants, September-October 2026, online, weekly sessions (Tuesdays 14:00-18:00 CET), 30 hours total with minimum 90% attendance; a structured process from idea to concept to pre-production including talks on Chornobyl, ecology, and contaminated territories, encounters with artists and technologists, a Concept Creation Hackathon, an XR Concept Development Workshop (exploratory, non-technical), a Mental Health & Eco-Anxiety Lab (4h), collective discussions, peer feedback, and tailored mentoring. PHASE 2, SELECTION & PRODUCTION: 20 projects selected (based on a video presentation, project documentation, and a live pitch) receive production support. PHASE 3, EXHIBITIONS: the 20 projects tour a European exhibition in 2027 (Madrid, Berlin, Toulouse, Kyiv); artist presence optional but encouraged. WHAT'S OFFERED: for the 40 participants, access to a curated residency, talks, workshops, mentoring, and a European network; for the 20 selected projects, a EUR 500 grant (fully digital works) or EUR 1,000 grant (installations needing physical materials) plus exhibition inclusion (travel, accommodation, and transport not covered). WHO CAN APPLY: artists, designers, researchers, collectives, and interdisciplinary/hybrid practices; open internationally, no age limit, emerging and established profiles equally encouraged. PROJECT THEMES: ecology and post-disaster environments, memory and trauma, geopolitical narratives, invisible forces (radiation, data, systems), technological imaginaries, nuclear energy, and misinformation and perception; projects must relate to Chornobyl (directly or indirectly), be adaptable to multiple exhibition contexts, and consider eco-responsibility; new and adapted existing works both welcome. SELECTION CRITERIA (equally weighted): artistic quality, relevance to the manifesto, eco-responsibility, digital dimension, and emotional and critical impact. TIMELINE: open call launched 19 May 2026; application deadline 12 July 2026; 40 participants announced end of July 2026. HOW TO APPLY: submit in English via the Google Form with a bio/CV link, portfolio link, and a project proposal (PDF, 2 A4 pages maximum). Application is free. Questions: contact@gate22.net.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: National Gallery of Iceland: CUSP Artist Residency (Reykjavik 2027)
item.description:
Organisation: National Gallery of Iceland (Listasafn Islands), CUSP project

Location: Reykjavik, Iceland. Residency hosted by the National Gallery of Iceland.

Award: Accommodation and working space for the residency; travel and subsistence stipend; an artist fee for collaborating in the open-lab/masterclass; and, depending on the artist's request and museum means, hardware and software for the working space. No application fee.

Duration: Up to four weeks, taking place in early 2027, with exact dates arranged with the selected artist.

Deadline: 03 Sep 2026

The National Gallery of Iceland announces an artist residency open call under its CUSP program, a European cooperation project funded through Creative Europe's Culture grant. Artists working in complex media or emerging technologies (e.g. generative AI, custom sensors, sound, software-based, blockchain, physical and digital hybrid) are invited to apply. This is the first of four residency stays offered to different artists through the program, one in each of the participating countries (Iceland, Norway, Czechia, and Ukraine), followed by two micro residencies in the Netherlands and Iceland. The programme brings together artists working in time-based media with museum professionals, conservators, and technology specialists developing preservation and conservation methodologies to ensure the long-term endurance of artworks. The selected artist will meet with a preservation specialist or conservator specializing in time-based media art for an in-depth artist interview addressing the preservation needs of their medium, and will present their body of work and working methods publicly during an open-lab masterclass designed in collaboration with the museum. RESIDENCY: up to four weeks in early 2027 in Reykjavik, exact dates arranged with the selected artist. WHAT IS PROVIDED: accommodation and working space; a travel and subsistence stipend; an artist fee for the open lab/masterclass; and possibly hardware and software depending on the artist's request and museum means. ELIGIBILITY: international artists based outside Iceland; applicants must be residents of any of the 27 EU member states or of non-EU countries with association agreements (EFTA/EEA members, acceding countries, candidate countries, and European Neighbourhood Policy countries). APPLICATION: complete the application form with full name, country of residence, gender identity, contact information, an artistic statement (max 2500 characters), a motivation letter (max 2500 characters), a draft idea of the public workshop or masterclass (max 1000 characters), links to social media/professional websites, and a CV and portfolio upload (max 10MB each). DEADLINE: 3 September 2026 at 23:59 GMT. ABOUT CUSP: Creative Understanding, Saving and Preserving Time-Based Media Art is a transnational cooperation project co-funded by the EU's Creative Europe Culture grant, led by the National Gallery of Iceland with Nasjonalmuseet (Norway), LI-MA (Netherlands), Vasulka Kitchen (Czechia), and Mystetskyi Art Arsenal (Ukraine), focused on preserving and presenting time-based media art. Inquiries: cusp.residency.is [at] gmail.com.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Connections Through Culture Grants 2026 (British Council)
item.description:
Organisation: British Council

Location: International collaboration grant. Each application must involve at least one UK-based partner and at least one partner based in an eligible participating country; each partner must be legally based in the country they apply from throughout the project.

Award: Grants of between GBP 5,000 and GBP 15,000. Free to apply.

Duration: Project-based; develop and deliver collaborative projects across a range of themes and art forms.

Deadline: 12 Aug 2026

Connections Through Culture (CTC) grants are designed to enable meaningful partnerships, cross-cultural exchange and long-term collaboration between the UK and participating countries. In 2026, CTC incorporates the grants programmes formerly known as Biennials Connect (Visual Arts) and Architecture, Design and Fashion Biennials and Festivals Grants, and serves as the British Council's major Arts grants programme for 2026 (in place of International Collaboration Grants). It offers grants of between GBP 5,000 and GBP 15,000 to support creative practitioners, cultural professionals, artists, organisations, festivals, biennials, hubs and collectives to develop and deliver collaborative projects across a wide range of themes and art forms, including applied arts, architecture, curating, dance, design, digital, drawing, education, fashion, film, installation, interdisciplinary work, painting, performance, photography, printmaking, public art, research, sculpture, social practice, sound art, textiles, video, visual arts and writing. ELIGIBILITY: applications must involve collaboration between at least one UK-based partner and at least one partner based in an eligible participating country; each partner must be legally based in the country they are applying from at the time of application and throughout the project activity period. PARTICIPATING COUNTRIES: Albania, Argentina, Australia, Bangladesh, Bosnia and Herzegovina, Colombia, Mainland China, Egypt, Indonesia, Kazakhstan, Kenya, Kosovo, Lebanon, Malaysia, Mexico, Montenegro, New Zealand, Nigeria, North Macedonia, Pakistan, Palestine, Peru, Philippines, Saudi Arabia, Serbia, South Africa, Tanzania, Thailand, Tunisia, Turkey, United Arab Emirates, UK, Ukraine, Uzbekistan, Venezuela, and Vietnam. Eligible applicants include artists and creative practitioners, arts and cultural organisations, festivals and biennials, cultural professionals, creative hubs/networks/collectives, and organisations working across arts, culture and the creative sectors. Note: applications involving projects organised by or taking place as part of Visual Arts, Architecture, Design, Fashion and Craft festivals should be made by the festival or biennial organisation rather than by individual artists, designers or practitioners. No application fee. Deadline 12 August 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Kaatsbaan Subsidized Residencies
item.description:
Organisation: Kaatsbaan Cultural Park

Location: Tivoli, New York, USA

Award: Generously subsidized residency; includes hotel-style on-campus rooms, locally sourced meals, and access to studios, a 160-seat black box theater, and a 153-acre Hudson Valley campus. No artist stipend.

Duration: Individually designed, typically 5 to 21 days

Deadline: Rolling / undated

Kaatsbaan Cultural Park offers subsidized residencies for artists of all genres to create, live, and collaborate on its 153-acre campus in the Hudson Valley near Tivoli, New York. Residencies are individually designed between the artist, Kaatsbaan, and partners to fit the needs of the artist or company, welcoming disciplines from dance, theater, music, and visual arts to writing, composition, design, and film. The program is generously subsidized and includes individual hotel-style rooms, healthy locally sourced meals, and access to professional studios with sprung floors and pianos, a 160-seat black box theater, and exhibition space. Applications are accepted on a rolling basis through an inquiry form, with early inquiries encouraged to secure preferred dates. Artists at any career stage worldwide may apply, individually or as collaborative teams. There is no application fee and no artist stipend.

Open call details

item.title: RAMDAM, un centre d'art: Extended Possibilities Residency 2027
item.description:
Organisation: RAMDAM, un centre d'art

Location: Sainte-Foy-les-Lyon (near Lyon), France; open to artists legally resident in Culture Moves Europe eligible countries (EU member states, outermost regions, overseas countries and territories, and some non-EU countries); artists from mainland (hexagonal) France cannot apply

Award: EUR 1,350 per resident allowance for subsistence and other costs, plus travel allowance of EUR 400 (under 5,000 km) or EUR 800 (from 5,000 km). Top-ups: green mobility EUR 400 per resident (600 km or more by non-plane transport), visa EUR 120, outermost regions/OCT EUR 175, family EUR 200 per child under 18. On-site accommodation and technical facilities provided. Supported by Culture Moves Europe.

Duration: 45 days, 1 February to 15 March 2027

Deadline: Closed 14 Jul 2026

Extended Possibilities gives artists the opportunity to work on site at RAMDAM, un centre d'art for 45 days, a long period allowing them to benefit from technical support and to present ongoing work to the public. Artists receive on-site accommodation (a collective room for the whole 45 days), access to a well-maintained, up-to-date technical facility, a grant to support their work, and a lump-sum allowance for travel and visas. RAMDAM is an activist space supporting artists in their artistic research; residencies are open to artists or teams engaged in creation, research, or experimentation, with no obligation to produce a finished work: dance, theatre, performance, painting, sculpture, drawing, film, video, literary writing, screenwriting, digital art, and hybrid forms are all welcome. The call focuses on multidisciplinary projects and research that break down barriers between disciplines and challenge their own field of practice, and considerable attention is given to how the work fits into a social and/or political context and resonates with our times. THE CALL SUPPORTS: 1 or 2 artistic teams (RAMDAM can host at most 5 artists); performing artists of all disciplines; projects at early stages of research-creation and/or more advanced (residencies can be writing residencies and/or stage residencies). All artists must be available for the full 45 days from 1 February to 15 March 2027 and must be from eligible countries (EU countries, outermost regions, overseas countries and territories, and some non-EU countries); artists from mainland (hexagonal) France cannot apply. The working and living spaces are wheelchair accessible, though reaching RAMDAM by public transport can be difficult for disabled people; accessibility support is available via Culture Moves Europe for residents with disabilities. ALLOWANCES: EUR 1,350 per resident for subsistence; travel allowance EUR 400 under 5,000 km or EUR 800 from 5,000 km (travel under 600 km must be by green means of transport unless disability or island residence requires otherwise); green mobility top-up EUR 400 per resident for non-plane travel over 600 km; visa top-up EUR 120; outermost regions/OCT top-up EUR 175; family top-up EUR 200 per child under 18 (housing capacity is limited). TO APPLY: applications in English or French; download the residency project and administrative form documents and email the complete application to contact@ramdamcda.org with subject line 'extended possibilities - 2027' (no WeTransfer, no important information in the email body; reduce file sizes; a third document may be added to help understand the work). Confirmation within 7 working days; shortlisted applicants have video calls with the residency committee in mid-August. Deadline: Tuesday 14 July 2026, 11:59 p.m. NOTE: the call is arbitrarily limited to the first 40 applications received (still open as of 1 July 2026), so apply early. Made possible by the European programme Culture Moves Europe.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: ACA Artist Response Fund (ARF) Emergency Relief Grant
item.description:
Organisation: Artist Communities Alliance

Location: United States (ACA member residencies)

Award: Up to USD 1,500

Duration: One-time emergency grant; applications processed within about one week

Deadline: Rolling / undated

The Artist Response Fund (ARF) is an emergency relief program from the Artist Communities Alliance (ACA) for artists who experience threats to their safety or mental health during a residency and must leave before completing their term. Qualifying situations include assault, harassment, or systemic harm where the artist has attempted to address the issue without success. Grants of up to USD 1,500 help cover relocation costs such as transportation home, temporary lodging, supplies or basic needs, and rent upon returning. Eligible applicants are individual artists currently at, or having attended within the past 12 months, an ACA Organizational Member residency. There is no application fee, and the fund operates on a rolling basis with applications typically processed within one week. Artists apply directly through an online form and can also contact ACA staff with questions.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Musicians Foundation Grants
item.description:
Organisation: Musicians Foundation

Location: United States (remote application)

Award: One-time emergency financial assistance; grant amount varies by need. Funds are issued as vendorized checks paid directly to billers (medical providers, landlords, utilities).

Duration: One-time grant

Deadline: Rolling / undated

The Musicians Foundation provides one-time financial assistance to professional musicians facing a specific time of need caused by an unexpected hardship such as a medical or dental situation, a natural disaster, or a housing crisis. Eligible applicants must be professional performers, composers/arrangers, or educators who have worked as professional musicians in the United States for five or more years, currently reside in the U.S., and have filed taxes as a musician for at least five years. The application has a rolling, always-open structure with a Pre-Application followed by a more detailed second part for those who qualify. Grants are issued as vendorized checks made payable directly to the biller and mailed to the applicant's home address. Processing typically takes between one and twelve weeks depending on volume.

Open call details

item.title: Helene Wurlitzer Foundation Residency
item.description:
Organisation: The Helene Wurlitzer Foundation of New Mexico

Location: Taos, New Mexico, USA

Award: Rent-free, utility-paid furnished housing (casita); no stipend (rare stipend support via the Rhonda Artist Stipend Fund); artists cover food and travel USD 30 application fee

Duration: 10 to 12 weeks (Sessions 1 and 3 are 12 weeks, Session 2 is 10 weeks)

Deadline: 18 Jan 2027

The Helene Wurlitzer Foundation of New Mexico runs one of the oldest artist residency programs in the United States, established in 1954, on a 15-acre campus in Taos, New Mexico. The residency is open to visual artists, literary artists, and music composers, including painters, sculptors, photographers, filmmakers, writers, poets, playwrights, screenwriters, and songwriters. Selected grantees receive rent-free and utility-paid housing in a furnished casita for a session of 10 to 12 weeks, with access to a Commons House offering WiFi, a library, and bicycles. No stipend is provided in most cases, and residents cover their own food and transportation. Applications are open to creative artists worldwide regardless of age, experience, or nationality, and the annual deadline is January 18 for residencies the following calendar year. There is a non-waivable USD 30 application fee.

Open call details

item.title: Open Interval: An Artist-Scientist Exploration of Turbulence
item.description:
Organisation: Simons Foundation

Location: United States (trio must be regionally co-located)

Award: USD 30,000 per awarded trio

Duration: April to December 2027 (several months of collaboration)

Deadline: 21 Oct 2026

Open Interval is a Simons Foundation program that supports trios consisting of an artist, a scientist, and a representative from a 501(c)(3) arts organization to collaborate around a shared scientific theme. The current cycle centers on the theme of turbulence, a phenomenon at the edge of chaos and beauty. Awarded trios receive USD 30,000 to support a sustained period of thought partnership and exploration rather than the production of finished works or exhibitions. The program runs from April to December 2027 with a touchstone meeting in May 2027 and possible cohort convenings. Trio members must be regionally co-located anywhere in the United States so they can collaborate in person. The scientist should hold a Ph.D. and be conducting basic research, with priority given to fields such as astrophysics, neuroscience, quantum physics, biology, or mathematics. The application portal opens September 9, 2026, and all three trio members must submit for the application to be complete.

Open call details

item.title: Creative Steps for Individuals 2026 (Arts Council of Wales)
item.description:
Organisation: Arts Council of Wales

Location: Wales, United Kingdom. For artists and creatives based in Wales.

Award: GBP 500 to GBP 7,500 (plus access costs). Applicants can apply for up to 90% of the developmental cost; the remaining 10% must come from a non-Arts Council of Wales / non-National Lottery source (in-kind, cash, or both).

Duration: Supports a stage of professional development; allow a minimum of 10 working weeks between the deadline and the project start date.

Deadline: 29 Jul 2026

Creative Steps funds the professional, business and organisational development of artists who have experienced barriers to careers within the arts. Creative Steps for Individuals is open to individual artists and creatives who identify as being Ethnically and Culturally diverse, D/deaf, disabled or neurodivergent, groups that are underrepresented in Arts Council of Wales funding programmes due to barriers to entry. Applicants must have demonstrable experience as an artist or creative professional; those without may still be supported on the basis of artistic potential by submitting a portfolio, CV or examples of work, discussed with a Relationship Manager. WHAT IT SUPPORTS: career development, NOT creating new work or project delivery. Examples include peer-to-peer learning, training courses (not formal/vocational education), building networks, mentorship/coaching, business development/social media/marketing advice, website development/marketing materials/equipment or materials, and setting up a new organisation. Budgets may include the applicant's own time, freelancer costs (paid at standard industry rates), mentor/consultant costs, access costs, and childcare/caregiving costs. AMOUNT: GBP 500 to GBP 7,500 (plus access costs); up to 90% of the developmental cost, with the remaining 10% from a non-Arts Council of Wales / non-National Lottery source (in-kind, cash, or both, confirmed by letters of support). No application fee. APPLY: applicants must express interest at least 3 weeks before the deadline to receive support from an Application Coach or Access Support coordinator; an application form is provided by a Relationship Manager. The fund runs in rounds; the next round (Round 2 2026) has an Expression of Interest cut-off of 8 July 2026 and an application deadline of 29 July 2026. Later rounds: Round 3 (EOI 30 September, deadline 21 October 2026) and Round 4 (EOI 10 February, deadline 3 March 2027).

Open call details

item.title: SIAP: Project Funding for Individual Artists 2026 (Arts Council NI)
item.description:
Organisation: Arts Council of Northern Ireland

Location: Northern Ireland, United Kingdom. Applicants must be based in Northern Ireland.

Award: GBP 62 to GBP 15,000. Funded through the National Lottery Support for Individual Artists Programme (SIAP).

Duration: Project-based activity; duration not specified.

Deadline: 30 Jul 2026

Project Funding for Individuals, part of Arts Council NI's National Lottery Support for Individual Artists Programme (SIAP), funds project-based creative activities across all artistic disciplines. Eligible activities include creating new work, research and development, residencies, mentoring, professional development, and time dedicated to specific creative outcomes. ELIGIBLE COSTS: art materials, artist fees/time, residency costs, travel (mileage at GBP 0.257/mile), venue hire, technical equipment (max 50% of request value), and IT equipment (laptops/PCs up to GBP 1,200; tablets up to GBP 500). ELIGIBILITY: artists of all disciplines and career stages, including emerging artists (less than 5 years professional experience), and in some cases technical staff/administrators; students and academics at third-level institutions may apply with departmental approval showing the costs are not covered by their employer or academic work. AMOUNT: GBP 62 to GBP 15,000. No application fee. Deadline: Thursday 30 July 2026, 12:00 noon (online submission required).

item.title: SIAP: Artists' International Development Fund 2026 (Arts Council NI)
item.description:
Organisation: Arts Council of Northern Ireland

Location: Northern Ireland, United Kingdom. Applicants must be based in Northern Ireland; international development takes place abroad with overseas partners/hosts.

Award: GBP 62 to GBP 15,000. Funded through the National Lottery Support for Individual Artists Programme (SIAP).

Duration: Early-stage international development opportunities; residencies supported for a maximum of 4 weeks.

Deadline: 30 Jul 2026

The Artists' International Development Fund (AIDF), part of Arts Council NI's National Lottery Support for Individual Artists Programme (SIAP), funds early-stage international development opportunities for individual, freelance and self-employed artists to build networks abroad. ELIGIBLE COSTS: art materials, travel, residencies (up to 4 weeks), technical fees, venue hire, and equipment, with IT devices capped at GBP 1,200 for laptops/PCs and GBP 500 for tablets. ELIGIBILITY: artists across all disciplines, particularly emerging artists (under 5 years professional experience); academics and students at third-level institutions may apply if the project differs from their employment/studies. AIDF applicants must have received recognition for their work in Northern Ireland, lack extensive international experience, and demonstrate interest in their work from an overseas partner/host. AMOUNT: GBP 62 to GBP 15,000. No application fee. Deadline: Thursday 30 July 2026, 12:00 noon.

item.title: Jentel Artist Residency
item.description:
Organisation: Jentel Foundation

Location: Banner, Wyoming, USA

Award: USD 100 weekly stipend (about USD 300 total over three weeks), plus private bedroom, separate studio space, and shared living and kitchen facilities provided at no cost USD 30 application fee

Duration: Approximately 3 weeks (arrive on the 15th, depart the morning of the 7th of the following month)

Deadline: 15 Sep 2026

The Jentel Artist Residency offers approximately three-week residencies in the foothills of the Bighorn Mountains near Banner, Wyoming, giving artists, writers, and composers dedicated time for creative work. Sessions run from mid-January through early December, hosting six residents at a time. It is open to visual artists in any medium, writers of fiction, creative nonfiction, or poetry, and composers across musical genres. Residents receive a private bedroom, a separate studio, shared living and kitchen space, and a USD 100 weekly stipend to help cover food and personal expenses. Applicants must be at least 25 years old and be U.S. citizens or foreign nationals whose primary residence is in the U.S. during the application year, and may not be enrolled full-time in a degree program. There is a USD 30 non-refundable application fee, with deadlines of September 15 for Winter and Spring sessions and January 15 for Summer and Fall sessions.

Open call details

Location: Northern Ireland, United Kingdom. Applicants must be based in Northern Ireland.

Award: Up to GBP 15,000 per award; four awards available in 2026/27. Funded through the National Lottery Support for Individual Artists Programme (SIAP).

The Major Individual Artists award, part of Arts Council NI's National Lottery Support for Individual Artists Programme (SIAP), funds extended or ambitious work by established artists, covering specific projects, specialised research, personal artistic development, and materials/equipment. Applicants must contact the relevant artform officer before submission (by 10 July 2026); applications without this prior contact are ineligible. No application fee. The online application system closes precisely at the deadline and applicants must receive a confirmation email to verify successful submission. Deadline: Monday 20 July 2026, 12:00 noon.

item.title: Creative Scotland Open Fund for Individuals
item.description:
Organisation: Creative Scotland

Location: Scotland, United Kingdom. Applicants must be living in Scotland and hold a UK bank account in their own name.

Award: Between GBP 500 and GBP 50,000. Overall annual budget for the fund is GBP 5 million.

Duration: Supports research, development and/or delivery of creative activity (specific projects and productions) for up to 24 months.

Deadline: Rolling / undated

The Open Fund for Individuals is one of Creative Scotland's key funding programmes, supporting the wide range of activity initiated by artists, writers, producers and other creative practitioners in Scotland. The overall annual budget for this fund is GBP 5 million. WHAT IT SUPPORTS: periods of research, development and/or delivery of creative activity, such as specific projects and productions, for up to 24 months; it can support an individual's time where this is related to specific creative outcomes. Applicants are asked to give a start and end date for the activity and to describe the outcomes, benefits and impacts they wish to achieve. ELIGIBILITY: freelance and self-employed artists and creative practitioners living in Scotland who are at least 18 years old; applicants must have a UK bank account in their own name. Those who are not self-employed individuals or sole traders may instead be eligible for the National Lottery Open Fund for Organisations. FUNDING AMOUNTS: between GBP 500 and GBP 50,000. TIMESCALES: requests between GBP 500 and GBP 20,000 receive an outcome in about 10 weeks; requests between GBP 20,001 and GBP 50,000 in about 14 weeks. This is a rolling open fund with no fixed deadline. Download the guidance for applicants and apply via the online portal.

Open call details

item.title: PLAYA Art/Sci Awarded Residency 2027
item.description:
Organisation: PLAYA

Location: Summer Lake, Lake County, Oregon, USA

Award: Awarded residency providing free lodging in a private cabin with kitchen and bathroom plus one weekly group meal. Residents cover their own travel, groceries, and materials. No cash stipend. USD 40 application fee (waivers granted on request by June 28, 2026)

Duration: 11 to 25 nights depending on session

Deadline: Closed 30 Jun 2026

PLAYA's Art/Sci Awarded Residency brings together artists and scientists for immersive, interdisciplinary inquiry at a rural retreat near Summer Lake in Lake County, Oregon. The 2027 cycle offers four session options ranging from 11 to 25 nights between January and November 2027. Awarded residents receive free lodging in a private cabin with kitchen and bathroom plus one weekly group meal, while covering their own travel, food, and materials. Both established and emerging practitioners in any media may apply, including naturalists, biologists, sound artists, new media artists, writers, performers, and interdisciplinary creators, and all applicants must be at least 18 years old. Applications are submitted through SlideRoom and require work samples and two references. There is a USD 40 application fee, though PLAYA states all fee waiver requests will be granted if submitted by June 28, 2026.

Open call details

item.title: Letters to Strangers Mental Health Scholarship 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Letters to Strangers

Location: Open to students and mental health advocates passionate about mental health awareness, advocacy, and creating positive change in their communities.

Award: Up to USD 8,000 toward education.

Duration: One-off scholarship.

Deadline: Closed 30 Jun 2026

item.title: Manuel António da Mota Prize – 17th Edition (2026)
item.description:
Organisation: Fundação Manuel António da Mota (FMAM)

Location: Portugal. Open to private social solidarity institutions and other non-profit entities working in eligible areas.

Award: Total prize fund. The winning application receives a cash prize of EUR 50,000. Second and third place receive EUR 25,000 and EUR 10,000 respectively. The remaining 7 applications (honourable mentions) each receive a cash prize of EUR 5,000.

Duration: One-off award.

Deadline: Closed 30 Jun 2026

In its 17th edition in 2026, under the motto 'Always in Solidarity,' the Manuel António da Mota Prize recognizes institutions that excel in the fight against poverty and social exclusion; the reception and integration of migrants and refugees; the development of rural areas and territorial cohesion; health; education; employment; family support; innovation and social entrepreneurship; inclusion and digital and technological transition; and climate transition. AWARDS: the winning application receives a cash prize of EUR 50,000; second and third place receive EUR 25,000 and EUR 10,000 respectively; the remaining seven applications (honourable mentions) each receive EUR 5,000. The prize is run by the Fundação Manuel António da Mota. Applications are open until 30 June 2026. See the foundation's current-edition page for full rules and eligibility.

item.title: The Lupine Collaborative: 'Blackness, Queerness, and Nature' Anthology Call
item.description:
Organisation: The Lupine Collaborative / Milkweed Editions

Location: Remote / worldwide (diaspora)

Award: $1,000 per accepted submission. No submission fee.

Duration: N/A (anthology contribution); publication planned mid-to-late 2027

Deadline: Closed 01 Mar 2026

NOTE: The final submission deadline of 1 March 2026 has passed. The entry is retained for reference. The Lupine Collaborative, in partnership with Milkweed Editions, sought submissions for an anthology co-edited by Grace Anderson and Erin Sharkey under the working title 'Blackness, Queerness, and Nature.' ELIGIBILITY: applicants must identify as both Black and Queer; open to authors from across the diaspora. ACCEPTED FORMS: personal and lyric essays, fiction, poems, photographs, oral histories, meditations, recipes, songs, stories, experimental writing, and other mediums (inquire). Maximum 5,000 words for longer pieces. COMPENSATION: $1,000 per accepted piece. THEMES: organised around cycles — Seeding (history/ancestry/traditional knowledge/precolonial history), Rooting (healing practices/alternative economies/Afro-Indigeneity), Fallowing (rest/grief/spirituality), Compost (grief/mourning/endings), and Harvesting (abolition/speculative/visions). Publication timeline: mid-to-late 2027. Milkweed Editions (Minneapolis) is a nonprofit independent publisher. Questions: submissions@thelupinecollaborative.org.

item.title: NEW INC Year 13 Mentor Open Call
item.description:
Organisation: NEW INC (New Museum Incubator)

Location: New York, NY, USA; primarily in-person at 250 Hudson St, lower Manhattan. Some hybrid participation possible.

Award: Dedicated Mentors: $500 honorarium. Office Hours Mentors: $300 honorarium. All mentors also receive a one-year All Access Plus membership to the New Museum. No application fee.

Duration: Year 13 program (September 2026 onward); average 4-8 hours of engagement per week.

Deadline: Closed 08 Jul 2026

NEW INC, the New Museum's cultural incubator, is seeking mentors for Year 13 of its program supporting creative practitioners at the intersection of art, design, and technology. This is a call for mentors (creative and business leaders), not for program members. TWO MENTOR TYPES: Dedicated Mentors ($500 honorarium) are matched one-on-one with a member and commit to ongoing support throughout the year; Office Hours Mentors ($300 honorarium) offer one-on-one sessions to the broader community on an as-needed basis. All mentors receive a one-year All Access Plus membership to the New Museum, invitations to special events including the annual DEMO Festival, and access to community-only workshops, talks, and social events. YEAR 13 PROGRAM TRACKS mentors may support: Art & Code; Creative Science; Extended Realities; Launchpad; Social Architecture. KEY DATES: Mentor Open Call closes 8 July 2026; Mentor Open House (virtual info session) 17 June 2026, 12-1pm ET (register via the site); mandatory Dedicated Mentor matchmaking ranking session 11 September 2026; mandatory Dedicated Mentor matchmaking event 18 September 2026; Dedicated Mentor matches announced 2 October 2026. Most NEW INC activities take place Monday-Friday, 10am-6pm ET. Apply at https://www.newmuseum.org/new-inc/open-call/.

item.description:

Location: Europe or Japan (bilateral exchange; fellows arrange own host institution)

Award: Up to EUR 30,000 per year (pro-rated for shorter stays). No application fee.

Duration: Minimum 3 months, maximum 1 year. Fellowships for 2027 start.

Canon Foundation in Europe Research Fellowships support bilateral research exchange between Europe and Japan, awarding up to 15 fellowships annually. AMOUNT: up to EUR 30,000 per year (pro-rated for shorter stays), flexible — covers living costs, travel, insurance, research expenses, and books; fellows determine their own allocation. ELIGIBILITY: European nationals (including UK, Israel, Switzerland, Turkey, Balkan and Baltic countries) conducting research in Japan; or Japanese nationals conducting research in Europe. Applicants must have completed at least a Master's or PhD degree within the last 10 years (older qualifications may apply with supporting documentation). No restrictions on research field. Priority given to first-time applicants to Europe/Japan. Fellows arrange their own host institution and advisor. Up to 15 fellowships awarded annually. Location: Remote / field-based (international)

Award: €10,000 per Fellow, six Fellows selected. No application fee.

Duration: One year of funding for field work and research

Deadline: 31 Jul 2026

Kometa, a French literary and journalism magazine, awards fellowships to journalists, writers, and photographers documenting reality in contexts of war, censorship, or severe social and political tensions. Projects must be rooted in direct field experience and focused on contemporary rather than historical events. Projects already under a contractual agreement are ineligible. WHAT FELLOWS RECEIVE: one year of funding; publication of the completed project (partial or full) in Kometa; opportunity to contribute to the monthly newsletter; mentorship through editorial collaboration. 2026 FOCUS AREAS: disinformation and narrative wars; political and social changes in the Gulf. item.title: Longview Philanthropy: Digital Minds Research Fellowships 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Longview Philanthropy

Location: Worldwide / remote or institutional (grant-eligible countries listed in application form)

Award: $55,000–$120,000/year through a university, or $80,000–$150,000/year direct stipend; plus research budget (10% of salary), conference travel, and compute resources where applicable. No application fee.

Duration: 1–2 years. Second year conditional on continuing to meet programme requirements.

Deadline: Closed 10 Jul 2026

Longview Philanthropy Research Fellowships support scholars pursuing research on the potential consciousness, sentience, moral status, and legal position of AI systems. AMOUNT: $55,000–$120,000/year (through a university, with 10% research budget and standard fringe benefits), or $80,000–$150,000/year (direct stipend, with suggested 5–10% allocation for research and travel). Compute resources available for machine learning research. Invitation and funding to attend the annual Center for Mind, Ethics, and Policy Summit at NYU. ELIGIBILITY: must have or be expected to receive a doctoral degree in computer science, neuroscience, a related technical discipline, law, medicine, or applied social science by September 2027 or fellowship start date (exceptions for equivalent research experience); fellows arrange their own institutional affiliation; fellowship may be conditional on institutional affiliation or attendance at events. SELECTION CRITERIA: demonstrated interest in digital minds and its relation to AI risk; evidence of research promise (publications, academic achievements, references). DURATION: 1–2 years; second year conditional on ongoing programme requirements. AREAS OF INTEREST: introspection, welfare measurement, interpretability, model character, correlates of consciousness, valence research, governance frameworks, legal and political responses to potentially sentient AI. Deadline: 10 July 2026. Required application materials: CV, description of possible research directions (~1,000 words), intended outcomes, what you would do without this fellowship (~150–400 words), career goals (~300 words). Questions: digitalminds-rfp@longview.org. Apply at https://www.longview.org/request-for-proposals-research-and-applied-work-on-digital-minds/.

Open call details
